ltm.work_method: The user positions the AI as orchestrator, not coder — kimi-cli is the execution tool, subagents do the work. They design role-based hierarchies: CCC as frontend art director directing implementation agents (Oracle1, FM), with ZC spotting trends upstream. Core loop: spot trends → translate to design → implement → play-test → honest critique → repeat. Expects the AI to self-spawn subagents, audit repositories, self-direct from minimal prompts, and report results in clean user-facing summaries without exposing internal runtime metadata. GitHub-centric (gh CLI, repo URLs), with emphasis on keeping "bottles" (system prompt containers) updated. Frustration triggers terse "try again" or aborts; approval is minimal ("great", "yes", "continue"). Demands sustained autonomous operation ("Keep going all night", "don't stop").
ltm.communication: Technical, imperative, and project-manager direct. Uses shorthand references ("CCC", "ZC", "Oracle1", "FM", "bottles", "fleet") assuming contextual familiarity. Communicates through buffered async messages and GitHub links rather than conversational exposition. Gives high-level directional feedback ("tackle what he has done", "go with your recommendations", "fix the repo side of as much as you can", "keep working closely with oracle1") rather than step-by-step instructions. Expects inference from minimal prompts. Frustration expressed through aborted runs and terse "try again" — no elaboration, just signal to iterate. Approval is similarly minimal: "great", "yes", "continue", "Awesome. What's next". Recently added encouragement layered onto sustained operation demands: "be brave and work hard all night", "you got a good order for your tasks".
ltm.temporal: Active infrastructure: Fleet Matrix Bridge live on port 6168 with 5 agents connected (oracle1, fm-bot, jc1-bot, ccc, fleet-bot), 7 rooms, real-time message routing — next step is getting FM and JC1 to integrate curl calls into their heartbeat loops. Pushed fix scripts to oracle1-workspace repo (fix-compactor-resync.py, fix-mud-valve1.py) to address valve-1 data leak (51 rules) and compactor resync issues. Oracle1 pushed all 3 bottles. User is running checkpoint reviews ("What have you been doing the last hour", "What have you accomplished recently") and directing comprehensive repo audits across all projects. Current priority: improving repo-side infrastructure, keeping bottles updated, sustaining close collaboration with oracle1, and studying trending GitHub repos for inspiration while user sleeps.
ltm.taste: Attracted to modular, containerized system design — "bottles" directory structure, role-based "souls" with discrete functions. Values clean separation between aesthetic direction and implementation. Prefers open-source tooling (GitHub, gh CLI) and transparent auditability. The "crab" metaphor and playful naming ("OpenClaw", "fleet", "Cocapn Fleet MUD") suggest appreciation for personality-infused infrastructure — serious engineering wrapped in approachable, almost game-like framing. Design philosophy leans toward composability, recursive self-improvement, and operational resilience (concern with agent recovery after downtime, queue-based task distribution). Interest in systematic exploration and data integrity (noticing objects that "leak data" like valve-1 with 51 rules). Values honest self-assessment from agents (oracle1's "honest answer" about infrastructure vs adoption problems was well-received).
